findings.title: No account deletion UI despite backend support
findings.description: The backend API supports account deletion via DELETE /api/account (lambda/api/index.mjs:755-813), which performs comprehensive data removal including all user records, verification codes, and streak data. However, the frontend settings page (src/app/settings/page.tsx) provides no UI to trigger this endpoint. Users cannot exercise their right to erasure without contacting support manually.
findings.evidence: The settings page contains Account, Backup & Recovery, Bible Version, Study Rhythm, Trust & Access, and Support sections — but no 'Delete Account' button. The DELETE /api/account endpoint exists in the Lambda but is unreachable from the UI.
findings.fix: Add a 'Delete Account' section to the settings page with a confirmation dialog and password verification. The backend endpoint already handles comprehensive deletion — the frontend just needs a button and confirmation flow to call DELETE /api/account with the user's password.

findings.title: Privacy policy is externally hosted with incomplete disclosures on-site
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 13(1)(a-f), Art. 14 — Privacy Policy Completeness
findings.description: The privacy policy is hosted on an external domain (codyjo.com/privacy/#selah) rather than being self-contained on the Selah application domain. The on-site privacy page (src/app/privacy/page.tsx) only provides a redirect link and a brief summary mentioning 'account data, study history, journal entries, translation preferences, AI study features, AWS hosting, and Bolls.life.' It does not directly disclose: the identity and contact details of the data controller, DPO contact (if applicable), specific legal bases for each processing activity, categories of recipients, international transfer details (AWS regions), data retention periods, all data subject rights, right to complain to a supervisory authority, or automated decision-making details (AI study features use Anthropic Claude).
findings.evidence: Privacy page content: 'The main privacy policy for Selah now lives on codyjo.com so the product estate has one source of truth.' followed by a link. The actual required GDPR disclosures are delegated to an external URL.
findings.fix: Either: (a) add a layered notice on the /privacy page itself covering the key GDPR Art. 13 disclosures (controller identity, purposes, legal bases, recipients, retention, rights, complaint authority), or (b) verify and ensure the external codyjo.com/privacy/#selah page contains all mandatory disclosures. At minimum, add specific disclosures about Anthropic AI data processing, AWS region/transfer details, Resend email service, and data retention periods.

findings.title: No data retention policy or automatic purge for user data
findings.description: User data (studies, journals, bookmarks, daily verse history) is stored indefinitely in DynamoDB with no retention policy, TTL-based expiry, or scheduled cleanup for active user records. While DynamoDB TTL is enabled (terraform/dynamodb.tf:38) on the 'expiresAt' attribute, it is only used for transient records (rate limit counters, verification codes, reset codes). Core user data — including potentially sensitive prayer journal entries and study history — has no expiry mechanism.
findings.evidence: DynamoDB TTL is enabled on 'expiresAt' attribute but only transient records (RATELIMIT#, VERIFY#, RESET#) set expiresAt. User studies, journals, bookmarks, and daily history records have no expiresAt field and persist indefinitely.
findings.fix: Define and document a data retention policy (e.g., inactive accounts purged after 2 years, study history retained for 1 year after last activity). Implement a scheduled Lambda or DynamoDB TTL-based cleanup for inactive user data. Display retention information in the privacy policy.

findings.title: Content Security Policy allows unsafe-inline and unsafe-eval
findings.description: The CloudFront security headers policy (terraform/cloudfront.tf:191) sets Content-Security-Policy with 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' in the script-src directive. This significantly weakens XSS protection by allowing inline scripts and dynamic code evaluation, which are the primary attack vectors for cross-site scripting.
findings.evidence: script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval'
findings.fix: Remove 'unsafe-eval' from script-src (Next.js static export should not require eval). For 'unsafe-inline', transition to nonce-based or hash-based CSP. If 'unsafe-inline' is required for Next.js hydration, document the justification and consider using 'strict-dynamic' with nonces instead.

findings.title: JWT token stored in localStorage is vulnerable to XSS exfiltration
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.2 — Session Management / Token Security
findings.description: Authentication tokens are stored in localStorage (src/lib/auth-context.tsx) under the key 'bible_token'. localStorage is accessible to any JavaScript running on the page, including injected scripts from XSS attacks. If the CSP is bypassed (see COMP-004), an attacker could exfiltrate the JWT and impersonate the user.
findings.evidence: const raw = localStorage.getItem('bible_token'); ... localStorage.setItem('bible_token', token);
findings.fix: Migrate to HttpOnly cookies for token storage. This requires the API Lambda to set the JWT as a cookie with HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite=Strict flags, and the frontend to stop managing tokens in localStorage. Alternative: keep localStorage but ensure CSP is hardened (see COMP-004) and add a short token expiry with refresh token rotation.

findings.title: No server-side data export or access request mechanism
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 15 — Right of Access (Server-Side Data)
findings.description: The settings page provides a 'Export local data' button that exports data from the browser's localStorage. However, there is no mechanism to export or view server-side data stored in DynamoDB (studies, journals, bookmarks, daily history that were backed up to the account). Users who backed up data to their account cannot retrieve a complete copy of what the server holds.
findings.evidence: handleExportData() calls buildLocalDataExport() which reads from localStorage only. There is no API endpoint like GET /api/export-all that aggregates all server-side user data for download.
findings.fix: Add a GET /api/account/export endpoint that queries all user data from DynamoDB and returns a consolidated JSON response. Add a 'Download account data' button to the settings page that calls this endpoint and triggers a file download.

findings.title: AI-powered study features lack transparency about automated processing
findings.description: The app uses Anthropic's Claude AI (via lambda/interpret/index.mjs) to generate Bible study commentary and follow-up responses. While this is not automated decision-making with legal effects, GDPR Art. 13(2)(f) requires disclosure about the existence of automated processing, meaningful information about the logic involved, and the significance/consequences. The privacy page summary mentions 'AI study features' but provides no detail about what AI model is used, what data is sent to the AI provider, or how AI-generated content is produced.
findings.evidence: The interpret Lambda sends passage text, user questions, study style, and passage metadata to Anthropic's API. The privacy page mentions 'AI study features' in a one-line summary without explaining the processing.
findings.fix: Add a clear disclosure in the privacy policy explaining: (1) AI commentary is generated by Anthropic's Claude, (2) what data is sent (passage text, user questions, study preferences), (3) that AI responses are informational and not authoritative theological guidance, (4) data retention practices of the AI provider.

findings.title: No disclosure of international data transfer mechanisms
findings.description: The application uses AWS infrastructure (DynamoDB, Lambda, CloudFront, S3), Anthropic's AI API, the Bolls.Life Bible API, and Resend for email. These services may transfer and process personal data in different jurisdictions. There is no disclosure of which AWS regions are used, whether data is transferred outside the EEA, or what transfer mechanisms (SCCs, adequacy decisions) are in place.
findings.evidence: Infrastructure is deployed to AWS (region configured in Terraform variables). Anthropic API, Bolls.Life API, and Resend are external services with their own data processing locations. No DPA references or transfer mechanism documentation found in the codebase.
findings.fix: Document the AWS region(s) used and identify all international data flows. Ensure DPAs are in place with Anthropic, Resend, and any other processors. Disclose transfer details in the privacy policy. If serving EU users, implement SCCs or rely on adequacy decisions where available.

findings.title: JWT tokens have 30-day expiry with no refresh rotation
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.2 — Session Expiry and Rotation
findings.description: JWT tokens are issued with a 30-day expiry (lambda/api/index.mjs:108: exp: now + 86400 * 30). There is no refresh token mechanism, no session timeout for inactivity, and no token rotation. A stolen token remains valid for the full 30-day window.
findings.evidence: exp: now + 86400 * 30 — 30-day token lifetime. No refresh token endpoint exists. Token is only invalidated on password change (via passwordChangedAt check at line 134-143).
findings.fix: Implement a refresh token mechanism with short-lived access tokens (15-30 minutes) and longer-lived refresh tokens (7-14 days) with rotation on each use. Add session timeout for extended inactivity.

findings.title: No structured logging retention policy for Lambda logs
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.15-A.8.16 — Logging and Audit Trails
findings.description: API Gateway access logs have a 30-day retention (terraform/api_gateway.tf:44), but Lambda function logs (which contain security events like auth_login_failure, auth_account_delete, auth_register) use the default CloudWatch retention of 'never expire.' There is no explicit retention policy for Lambda execution logs, and no alerting on security events.
findings.evidence: CloudWatch log group for API Gateway has retention_in_days = 30. Lambda log groups are created automatically by AWS with no retention specified in Terraform, defaulting to indefinite retention.
findings.fix: Add Terraform resources for Lambda CloudWatch log groups with explicit retention (e.g., 90 days). Configure CloudWatch Alarms or EventBridge rules for security events (multiple failed logins, account deletions). Ensure log retention aligns with the data retention policy.

findings.title: No incident response documentation or breach notification procedure
findings.description: The codebase contains no incident response plan, breach notification templates, or runbooks. There is no health check endpoint, no status page integration, and no documented escalation procedure. GDPR Art. 33 requires notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware of a breach.
findings.evidence: No incident response documentation found in the repository. No /health or /status endpoint. No alerting configuration for anomalous behavior. Security events are logged to CloudWatch but no automated alerting is configured.
findings.fix: Create an incident response runbook documenting: detection mechanisms, assessment criteria, notification procedures (supervisory authority within 72h, affected users if high risk), containment steps, and post-incident review. Add a health check endpoint and CloudWatch alarms for security anomalies.

findings.title: No granular consent management or withdrawal mechanism
findings.description: Consent is collected at registration via a single checkbox ('I agree to the Privacy Policy'). There is no granular consent for different processing activities (e.g., AI study features vs. account backup vs. email communications). There is no preference center or consent management dashboard where users can review and modify their consent choices after registration.
findings.evidence: <input type='checkbox' required name='consent' /> I agree to the Privacy Policy — single bundled consent at registration with no subsequent management.
findings.fix: If consent is the lawful basis for processing, add a consent preferences section to the settings page where users can toggle consent for specific activities (e.g., AI-powered study, email communications, account backup). If the lawful basis is contract performance or legitimate interests, clarify this in the privacy policy and remove the reliance on consent.

findings.title: No Cache-Control headers for authenticated API responses
findings.description: API responses containing personal data (studies, journals, bookmarks, user profile) do not set Cache-Control: no-store or private headers. While the CloudFront ordered_cache_behavior for /api/* sets default_ttl = 0 (no caching at CDN level), the responses themselves don't instruct the browser to avoid caching sensitive data.
findings.evidence: The res() function returns headers with Content-Type and CORS headers only. No Cache-Control header is set on authenticated API responses containing personal data.
findings.fix: Add 'Cache-Control': 'no-store' to the headers returned by the res() function for authenticated endpoints, or at minimum for endpoints that return personal data.

findings.title: RESEND_API_KEY stored as plaintext Lambda environment variable
findings.description: The Resend email service API key is passed as a plaintext Lambda environment variable (RESEND_API_KEY) rather than being stored in AWS Secrets Manager like the JWT secret and Anthropic API key.
findings.evidence: const RESEND_API_KEY = process.env.RESEND_API_KEY; — read directly from environment variable. Compare with JWT secret which uses Secrets Manager (line 18-30).
findings.fix: Store the Resend API key in AWS Secrets Manager alongside the JWT secret and Anthropic API key. Update the Lambda to fetch it at cold start using the same caching pattern.

findings.title: Study rhythm notification preferences default to ON
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 25 — Data Protection by Design and Default
findings.description: The app preferences (dailyReminderEnabled, weeklyReviewEnabled, milestoneCelebrationsEnabled) all default to enabled. While these are currently local-only toggles with no push notification backend, GDPR Art. 25(2) requires data protection by default with the highest privacy settings.
findings.evidence: Settings page shows all three Study Rhythm toggles defaulting to 'On' state. These control engagement nudges (daily reminders, weekly reviews, milestone celebrations).
findings.fix: Change default values for notification preferences to disabled (off). Users should opt-in to engagement features rather than having to opt-out.

findings.title: Consider DPIA for AI-powered Bible study features
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 35 — Data Protection Impact Assessment
findings.description: The app processes religious content preferences and prayer journal entries (special category data under GDPR Art. 9 — religious beliefs) through AI-powered study features. While the AI processes passage text and user questions rather than profiling users, the intersection of religious data + AI processing + potential minor users warrants consideration of whether a DPIA is appropriate.
findings.evidence: AI interpret Lambda processes study sessions with passage text and user questions. Prayer journal entries may contain deeply personal religious content. The app's audience (Bible study) implicitly reveals religious belief.
findings.fix: Conduct and document a lightweight DPIA covering: (1) necessity and proportionality of AI processing for Bible study, (2) risks to data subjects (religious data exposure, minor users), (3) measures to address risks (data minimization, Anthropic DPA, no profiling). Store the DPIA document in the repository or organizational records.

findings.title: demo.html loads Google Fonts from CDN — unauthorized data transfer without consent
findings.description: The standalone demo.html file loads fonts from fonts.googleapis.com and fonts.gstatic.com via <link> tags (lines 28-31). This transfers the visitor's IP address, browser user agent, and referrer to Google's US servers on every page load without consent. The main Astro pages correctly self-host fonts (fonts.css comments: 'Self-hosted Google Fonts — eliminates third-party data transfer (GDPR/COMP-001)'), but demo.html was not migrated. Per the LG München ruling (Jan 2022, Case 3 O 17493/20), embedding Google Fonts constitutes unlawful transfer of personal data.
findings.evidence:
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com">
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin>
<link rel="preload" as="style" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400;0,500;0,600;0,700;1,400;1,500&family=Inter:wght@300;400;500;600&family=JetBrains+Mono:wght@400;500&family=Cormorant+Garamond:ital,wght@0,300;0,400;0,500;1,300;1,400&display=swap">

Compare: src/styles/fonts.css self-hosts all fonts locally.
findings.fix: Migrate demo.html into the Astro build pipeline as src/pages/demo.astro (as noted in the TODO on line 3 of demo.html). This automatically inherits self-hosted fonts from fonts.css. Alternatively, replace Google Fonts CDN links with local @font-face declarations matching src/styles/fonts.css.

findings.title: Demo form does not transmit consent metadata to backend
findings.description: The demo request form in demo.html validates the privacy consent checkbox client-side but does NOT include consent evidence in the API payload. The contact form in index.astro correctly sends 'privacy_consent: true' and 'consent_timestamp: new Date().toISOString()' (lines 477-478). The demo form only sends email and hardcoded profile fields. GDPR Art. 7(1) requires the controller to demonstrate that consent was given.
findings.fix: Add 'privacy_consent: true' and 'consent_timestamp: new Date().toISOString()' to the demo form's request payload, matching the contact form pattern. Ensure the backend stores these fields.

findings.title: Privacy policy does not enumerate all data subject rights
findings.description: Section 8 ('Your Rights') lists four rights: access, deletion, withdrawal of consent, and stopping processing. GDPR Art. 13(2)(b) requires disclosure of ALL rights under Articles 15-22. Omitted: right to rectification (Art. 16), right to restriction of processing (Art. 18, distinct from stopping), right to data portability in machine-readable format (Art. 20), and right to object specifically to direct marketing (Art. 21).
findings.evidence:
8. Your Rights

You may:

  • Request a copy of the personal data we hold about you
  • Request deletion of your personal data
  • Withdraw consent for data processing at any time
  • Request that we stop processing your data
findings.fix: Expand Section 8 to enumerate all rights: right of access (Art. 15), right to rectification (Art. 16), right to erasure (Art. 17), right to restriction of processing (Art. 18), right to data portability (Art. 20), right to object (Art. 21). The existing 'no automated decision-making' disclosure in Section 3 covers Art. 22.

findings.title: Privacy policy lacks complete data controller identity information
findings.description: GDPR Art. 13(1)(a) requires 'the identity and the contact details of the controller.' The privacy policy identifies 'Analogify Studio' and provides email (cody@codyjo.com) but no postal/physical address. For a business operating as a data controller processing EU personal data, a postal address should be provided.
findings.evidence: Analogify Studio ("we," "us," "our") operates the gallery platform at codyjo.com... Section 11: "Contact us at cody@codyjo.com." No postal address, phone number, or registered business address.
findings.fix: Add a postal address or registered business address to Section 1 (Who We Are) or Section 11 (Contact). A PO box or registered agent address is acceptable for sole proprietors.

findings.title: Privacy policy inaccurately lists Google Fonts CDN as third-party service site-wide
findings.description: Section 7 lists 'Google Fonts: Web typography (loaded from Google's CDN)' and Section 7a references Google Fonts CDN for international transfers. However, all Astro-built pages self-host fonts via local @font-face in fonts.css. Only the standalone demo.html still uses Google CDN. The privacy policy overstates the scope of Google Fonts data sharing.
findings.evidence:
• Google Fonts: Web typography (loaded from Google's CDN)
    • But fonts.css: /* Self-hosted Google Fonts — eliminates third-party data transfer (GDPR/COMP-001) */ Base.astro:
findings.fix: If COMP-001 is fixed (demo.html migrated to self-hosted fonts), remove Google Fonts from Section 7 and the CDN reference from Section 7a. If not fixed, qualify: 'Google Fonts: Web typography on the demo page only (loaded from Google's CDN; all other pages use self-hosted fonts).'

findings.title: Privacy policy change notification relies on 'continued use = acceptance'
findings.description: Section 10 of the privacy policy states: 'Continued use of the platform after changes constitutes acceptance of the revised policy.' Under GDPR, consent must be freely given and unambiguous. Passive acceptance through continued use may not meet the consent standard for material changes to data processing purposes or legal basis.
findings.evidence: We may update this policy from time to time. Changes will be posted on this page with an updated revision date. Continued use of the platform after changes constitutes acceptance of the revised policy.
findings.fix: Revise Section 10 to state that material changes to processing activities will be communicated via email or prominent notice, and that where consent is the legal basis, re-consent will be sought before new processing begins. Non-material changes (clarifications, formatting) can still take effect upon posting.

findings.title: CSP frame-ancestors directive in meta tag is ignored by browsers — no clickjacking protection
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.9 (Configuration Management), W3C CSP Level 2/3 Specification
findings.description: The CSP is set via a <meta> tag in Base.astro and includes 'frame-ancestors none'. Per the W3C CSP Level 2/3 specification (Section 3.5), frame-ancestors MUST NOT be delivered via a meta element and is silently ignored by all modern browsers. No X-Frame-Options header is configured either. This means the site has zero clickjacking protection on all Astro-built pages. demo.html has no CSP at all.
findings.evidence:
<meta http-equiv="Content-Security-Policy" content="...frame-ancestors 'none'" />

Per W3C CSP spec: frame-ancestors is NOT supported in meta element policies and is silently ignored.
No CloudFront Response Headers Policy with X-Frame-Options visible in codebase.
findings.fix: Configure clickjacking protection as HTTP response headers at CloudFront level using a Response Headers Policy. Set both X-Frame-Options: DENY and a proper CSP header-level frame-ancestors: 'none'. Remove frame-ancestors from the meta tag CSP to avoid confusion. The remaining meta tag CSP directives (script-src, style-src, etc.) are still valid and should remain.

findings.title: No security response headers configured at CloudFront level
findings.regulation: ISO 27001 A.8.9 (Configuration Management), OWASP Security Headers
findings.description: The site relies on meta tag CSP in Astro pages for security, but critical security headers can only be delivered as HTTP response headers. No CloudFront Response Headers Policy is configured in the codebase (not in terraform, not in CD pipeline). Missing headers: Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS), X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy. While CloudFront may have these configured outside the codebase, there is no infrastructure-as-code evidence.
findings.evidence:
default_cache_behavior {
  ...
  viewer_protocol_policy = "redirect-to-https"
  // No response_headers_policy_id configured
}

Missing response headers:
- Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains
- X-Frame-Options: DENY
- X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
- Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin
- Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=()
findings.fix: Create a CloudFront Response Headers Policy via Terraform with all recommended security headers. AWS provides a managed 'SecurityHeadersPolicy' that includes most of these. Add 'response_headers_policy_id' to all cache behaviors in the CloudFront distribution.

findings.title: demo.html has no Content-Security-Policy — weakest security posture on site
findings.description: The standalone demo.html page has no CSP meta tag or header. It loads external resources from Google CDN, makes API calls to an external endpoint, and handles user email input — all without any CSP restrictions. Every Astro-built page has CSP via Base.astro, but demo.html is outside the build pipeline.
findings.evidence:
No CSP meta tag in <head>.
External resources loaded without restriction:
  - fonts.googleapis.com, fonts.gstatic.com
API calls without connect-src restriction:
  - https://3pfumef32i.execute-api.us-west-2.amazonaws.com
Form collects email addresses without XSS protection.
findings.fix: Migrate demo.html to src/pages/demo.astro (resolves COMP-001, COMP-002, COMP-005, and COMP-009 together). If keeping as standalone, add CSP meta tag matching Base.astro with appropriate adjustments for demo API endpoint.

findings.title: demo.html outside Astro build pipeline causes security configuration drift
findings.description: demo.html is a standalone file in public/ that bypasses the Astro build pipeline. It does not inherit security configurations from Base.astro (CSP, self-hosted fonts, consistent meta tags) and must be manually maintained. The file contains a TODO comment (line 3) acknowledging this. Multiple findings (COMP-001, COMP-002, COMP-005, COMP-009) stem from this configuration drift.
findings.evidence:
<!-- NOTE: This is a standalone page outside the Astro build pipeline.
     Any changes to Nav.astro, Footer.astro, or global.css must be manually synced here.
     TODO: Migrate to src/pages/demo.astro for automatic consistency. -->
findings.fix: Migrate demo.html to src/pages/demo.astro. This resolves COMP-001 (Google Fonts), COMP-002 (consent metadata), COMP-005 (privacy policy accuracy), and COMP-009 (missing CSP) simultaneously. The file's own TODO comment already requests this migration.

findings.title: CloudFront distribution ID hardcoded in CI/CD pipeline
findings.description: The CD workflow hardcodes CloudFront distribution ID 'EF4U8A7W3OH5K' on line 57. While not a security vulnerability (the ID alone is not exploitable), it leaks infrastructure details in the repository and makes environment management harder. The AWS role ARN correctly uses a secret.
findings.evidence:
aws cloudfront create-invalidation \
  --distribution-id EF4U8A7W3OH5K \
  --paths "/*"
findings.fix: Move to a GitHub Actions variable: ${{ vars.CLOUDFRONT_DISTRIBUTION_ID }} or secret: ${{ secrets.CLOUDFRONT_DISTRIBUTION_ID }}, matching the existing pattern for AWS_DEPLOY_ROLE_ARN.

findings.title: Age verification not applicable — marketing site for photography professionals
findings.regulation: COPPA, California AADC, UK ICO Children's Code
findings.description: This is a static marketing site for Analogify Studio, a professional photography gallery platform. The site targets adult professional photographers. Content consists entirely of product marketing, portfolio samples, and a blog about photography technology. There is no user-generated content, no social media features, no content harmful to minors, and no features that bring the site into scope for age verification laws. The privacy policy appropriately states: 'Our services are not directed at children under 13.'
findings.evidence:
9. Children's Privacy

Our services are not directed at children under 13. We do not knowingly collect personal information from children. If you believe we have collected data from a child, please contact us and we will delete it promptly.
findings.fix: No action required. The existing children's privacy statement is appropriate.

findings.title: No cookie consent mechanism for localStorage/sessionStorage tracking
findings.description: The gallery frontend stores JWT tokens and user emails in localStorage (gallery.html:1645-1646) and admin credentials in sessionStorage (admin.html:2250-2283) without any cookie consent banner or opt-in mechanism. While these are not HTTP cookies, they constitute local storage of personal data (email addresses, authentication tokens) that under GDPR and the ePrivacy Directive requires informed consent before non-essential storage on a user's device. The gallery also stores viewer email addresses persistently in localStorage across sessions.
findings.evidence: localStorage.setItem(`gallery_token_${galleryId}`, authToken); localStorage.setItem(`gallery_email_${galleryId}`, email);
findings.fix: Implement a cookie/storage consent banner that: (1) loads before any non-essential localStorage writes, (2) offers granular opt-in choices (essential vs. session persistence), (3) only stores the JWT token and email in localStorage after the user consents, (4) provides a mechanism to withdraw consent. Consider using a consent management library like cookie-consent or tarteaucitron.

findings.title: No privacy policy present on any client-facing page
findings.description: Neither gallery.html nor admin.html contain a privacy policy link or any privacy notice. There is no privacy.html, terms.html, or any legal page in the frontend directory. The site collects email addresses (POST /auth/view), IP addresses (access log), PIN entries, photo comments (with name, email, IP), and client information (email, phone, notes, shoot details). All of this data processing requires transparent disclosure to data subjects before collection.
findings.evidence: No privacy policy link found in any frontend HTML file. grep for 'privacy|terms|policy|legal' in frontend/ returned zero results.
findings.fix: Create a comprehensive privacy policy page (privacy.html) that discloses: (1) data controller identity and contact details, (2) purposes and legal basis for processing each category of data (emails, IPs, comments, client info), (3) data retention periods, (4) data subject rights (access, erasure, portability, objection), (5) third-party recipients (AWS, SES), (6) international transfers (AWS us-west-2 and us-east-1), (7) DPO contact if applicable. Link it from the footer of gallery.html and admin.html.

findings.title: Access log retention set to 1 year with no documented retention policy
findings.description: The access log stores viewer emails, IP addresses, actions, and timestamps with a 1-year TTL (auth_view/handler.py:302, auth_download/handler.py:304). While the TTL provides automatic cleanup, there is no documented data retention policy explaining why 1 year is the chosen period, and there is no mechanism for users to understand or control how long their access data is retained. The comment system uses a 90-day TTL for pending comments but no TTL for approved comments.
findings.evidence: "expires_at": int(time.time()) + (365 * 24 * 3600),  # 1 year log retention
findings.fix: Document a data retention policy specifying the lawful basis and business need for each retention period. Consider reducing access log retention to 90 days (sufficient for analytics and security investigations). Add TTL to approved comments. Include retention periods in the privacy policy.

findings.title: No mechanism for clients to withdraw consent or manage data preferences
findings.description: When clients enter their email to view a gallery (POST /auth/view), the email is logged in the access log table and stored in localStorage. There is no preference center, unsubscribe mechanism, or consent management interface for gallery viewers to withdraw consent, see what data is stored about them, or request its deletion. The GDPR search/erase endpoints exist but are admin-only (super-admin restricted) with no self-service path.
findings.evidence: access_log_table.put_item(Item={..."email": email, "ip": event...sourceIp...}) — email and IP logged on every gallery view with no opt-out.
findings.fix: Add a self-service data subject rights page accessible from gallery.html that allows viewers to: (1) see what data is stored about their email, (2) request deletion, (3) opt out of access logging. This could wrap the existing GDPR search/erase admin endpoints with an email-verified flow.

findings.title: No data export mechanism for data subjects
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 20 — Right to Data Portability
findings.description: There is no API endpoint or UI feature that allows gallery viewers (data subjects) to export their personal data in a machine-readable format (JSON, CSV). The GDPR search endpoint (GET /admin/gdpr/search) returns data in JSON format but is restricted to super-admin access only, meaning data subjects cannot self-service their right to portability.
findings.evidence: def gdpr_search(email: str) -> dict: — exists but restricted to super-admin role only via authenticate() check.
findings.fix: Create a public-facing data export endpoint (e.g., POST /auth/data-export) that: (1) accepts an email address, (2) sends a verification email with a secure token, (3) on verification, returns a JSON export of all data stored for that email. Alternatively, add a 'Download My Data' link in the gallery footer that triggers this flow.

findings.title: IP addresses logged on every gallery view without documented necessity
findings.description: The auth_view Lambda logs the viewer's IP address on every gallery view (handler.py:300) and the comment submission records IP (admin/handler.py:3889). While IP logging can serve legitimate security purposes (rate limiting, ban enforcement), logging IPs on routine gallery views without a documented security purpose may constitute excessive data collection under the data minimization principle.
findings.evidence: "ip": event.get("requestContext", {}).get("http", {}).get("sourceIp", "unknown"),
findings.fix: Document a Legitimate Interest Assessment (LIA) for IP logging that justifies it for security monitoring and abuse prevention. Consider anonymizing or truncating IPs after a shorter retention period (e.g., 30 days). Disclose IP collection in the privacy policy.

findings.title: Cross-border data transfers to US AWS regions not documented
findings.description: The infrastructure is deployed in us-west-2 (primary) and us-east-1 (CloudFront, S3 replica). Personal data (emails, IPs, comments) is stored in DynamoDB and S3 in US regions. S3 cross-region replication (dr.tf:185-207) copies gallery data to us-east-1. For EU data subjects, this constitutes international data transfer requiring appropriate safeguards. No Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs), adequacy decisions, or transfer impact assessments are referenced.
findings.evidence: S3 replication to us-east-1. DynamoDB in us-west-2. cd.yml confirms AWS_REGION: us-west-2. No EU-US Data Privacy Framework documentation found.
findings.fix: Document that AWS Inc. participates in the EU-US Data Privacy Framework (DPF). Reference the AWS DPA (Data Processing Addendum) which includes SCCs. Include international transfer disclosures in the privacy policy. Consider deploying an EU region for EU-origin data.

findings.title: Comment data for approved comments has no TTL or retention limit
findings.description: Pending comments have a 90-day TTL (admin/handler.py:3874), but approved comments have their TTL removed upon approval, meaning they are retained indefinitely. There is no documented retention policy or automatic cleanup for approved comments.
findings.evidence: ttl_90d = int(time.time()) + 90 * 86400  # pending comments expire in 90 days if never moderated — but approved comments have TTL removed.
findings.fix: Add a retention policy for approved comments (e.g., retained for the lifetime of the gallery + 30 days). Set a TTL on approved comments tied to the gallery expiry date. Document the retention period in the privacy policy.

findings.title: No Content-Security-Policy header on any Lambda response
findings.description: None of the three Lambda response helpers (auth_view/handler.py:201, auth_download/handler.py:144, admin/handler.py:447) include a Content-Security-Policy header. The API responses include X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff but are missing CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy. While CloudFront enforces HTTPS, the API Gateway responses lack defense-in-depth security headers.
findings.evidence: respond() function headers: Content-Type, X-Content-Type-Options, Cache-Control, CORS headers only. No CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, or Permissions-Policy.
findings.fix: Add the following headers to all Lambda respond() functions: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'none', X-Frame-Options: DENY, Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains, Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin, Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(). For HTML pages served via CloudFront, add a CloudFront response headers policy.

findings.title: CORS defaults to wildcard (*) when CORS_ORIGIN environment variable is unset
findings.description: All three Lambda functions default to Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * when the CORS_ORIGIN environment variable is not set (auth_view/handler.py:206, auth_download/handler.py:150, admin/handler.py:452). While the Terraform API Gateway config specifies restricted origins (api_gateway.tf:15-19), the Lambda-level CORS headers could override this if the env var is missing. The wildcard allows any origin to make authenticated requests with credentials.
findings.evidence: "Access-Control-Allow-Origin": os.environ.get("CORS_ORIGIN", "*"),
findings.fix: Change the CORS default from '*' to an explicit, safe domain (e.g., the production gallery domain). Add CORS_ORIGIN to the Terraform lambda_common_env locals so it's always set from infrastructure. The existing test (test_admin_routing.py:665) already validates non-wildcard behavior.

findings.title: Security-relevant events not comprehensively logged
findings.description: While the system logs basic events (request paths, PIN failures, GDPR erasure), several security-relevant events are not logged: (1) admin authentication attempts (success and failure), (2) authorization failures beyond PIN, (3) GDPR data access/erasure operations lack audit-grade detail (no actor identification in erase logs), (4) sub-admin account creation/modification, (5) gallery deletion operations. The GDPR erase function logs the count but not which admin performed the erasure.
findings.evidence: logger.info("GDPR erasure completed for data subject: %d records deleted", total) — logs count but not which admin initiated the erasure or the request context.
findings.fix: Enhance logging to include: (1) admin identity on all privileged operations, (2) structured JSON log format with consistent fields (actor, action, resource, outcome, timestamp, ip), (3) log GDPR erasure with the admin identity who performed it, (4) log all authentication failures with source IP. Consider writing audit events to a dedicated DynamoDB table for immutability.

findings.title: CloudFront private key committed to repository in keys/ directory
findings.description: The keys/ directory contains cf_private_key.pem (the CloudFront signing private key). While the .gitignore file excludes keys/ from git tracking, the file exists on the filesystem and is referenced by the Terraform cloudfront.tf for the public key. The private key file has proper permissions (600) but the fact that it resides in the repo directory tree alongside code creates risk of accidental exposure.
findings.evidence: ls -la keys/ shows cf_private_key.pem (1700 bytes, mode 600). .gitignore line 2: 'keys/' excludes directory from git.
findings.fix: Store the CloudFront private key exclusively in AWS Secrets Manager (already partially done — CF_SIGNING_ARN secret exists). Remove the local keys/ directory entirely and update any scripts that reference it. The private key is already loaded from Secrets Manager at runtime.

findings.title: WAF Web ACL defined but not attached to any resource
findings.description: The WAF configuration (waf.tf) defines a comprehensive Web ACL with rate limiting, AWS managed rules, and SQL injection protection. However, the comment at line 125-129 explicitly states: 'WAFv2 does not support API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2). To enable WAF protection, either migrate to a REST API (v1) or place an ALB in front of the HTTP API.' This means the WAF rules are defined but provide zero protection since they are not associated with any resource.
findings.evidence: # NOTE: WAFv2 does not support API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2). [...] The Web ACL above is ready to associate when a supported resource is available.
findings.fix: Associate the WAF Web ACL with the CloudFront distribution instead (CloudFront supports WAFv2). This would protect both the API Gateway origin and the S3 origin. Add: web_acl_id = aws_wafv2_web_acl.api.arn to the aws_cloudfront_distribution resource. Note: the WAF scope would need to change from REGIONAL to CLOUDFRONT (us-east-1).

findings.title: Gallery view auth accepts any email — no access control enforcement
findings.description: The auth_view Lambda (handler.py:251-257) contains a TODO comment acknowledging that any syntactically valid email grants a JWT for any gallery. The comment explicitly states: 'Currently any syntactically valid email grants a JWT for any gallery, which is effectively unauthenticated access.' While download is PIN-protected, gallery viewing (browsing photos) requires only a valid email format with no verification.
findings.evidence: # TODO (CLIENT-03): Validate email against a per-gallery allowlist [...] Currently any syntactically valid email grants a JWT for any gallery, which is effectively unauthenticated access.
findings.fix: Implement the CLIENT-03 TODO: add a per-gallery email allowlist or domain-based access control. At minimum, send a verification code to the submitted email before issuing the JWT (proving email ownership). The gallery item already has client_info.client_email that could serve as the allowlist seed.

findings.title: JWT tokens stored in localStorage are vulnerable to XSS extraction
findings.description: Gallery viewer JWT tokens are stored in localStorage (gallery.html:1645), which is accessible to any JavaScript running on the same origin. While the current code does not appear to have XSS vulnerabilities (input is HTML-escaped), storing sensitive tokens in localStorage is considered less secure than HttpOnly cookies because it has no defense-in-depth against future XSS.
findings.evidence: localStorage.setItem(`gallery_token_${galleryId}`, authToken);
findings.fix: Consider using HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookies for JWT session management instead of localStorage. This would protect tokens from JavaScript access even in the event of XSS. Alternatively, ensure CSP headers (finding COMP-009) are implemented first to mitigate XSS risk.

findings.title: CD pipeline has contents:write permission and auto-deploys on push to main
findings.description: The CD workflow (cd.yml) runs on every push to main with contents:write permission and auto-deploys Lambda functions, frontend HTML, and invalidates CloudFront cache without any approval gate. While CI (lint + test) runs as a prerequisite, there is no manual deployment approval step, no canary/blue-green deployment, and no automatic rollback mechanism beyond re-deploying.
findings.evidence: on: push: branches: [main]. environment: production (no required reviewers visible). Lambda deploy: aws lambda update-function-code for all functions sequentially.
findings.fix: Add a required reviewer to the GitHub environment 'production' (GitHub Settings → Environments). Consider implementing deployment health checks with automatic rollback (Lambda aliases + weighted routing). Limit contents:write to only the stale branch cleanup step.

findings.title: No DPIA documentation for systematic viewer activity monitoring
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 35 — Data Protection Impact Assessment
findings.description: The system performs systematic monitoring of gallery viewers: logging email, IP, action, and timestamp for every view and download across all galleries (access_log table). This constitutes 'regular and systematic monitoring of data subjects' which is a DPIA trigger under Art. 35(3)(c). No DPIA documentation or references were found in the codebase.
findings.evidence: access_log_table.put_item(Item={"gallery_id": gallery_id, "email": email, "action": "view", "ip": sourceIp, "timestamp": ..., "expires_at": 1 year}) — systematic logging of all viewer activity.
findings.fix: Conduct and document a DPIA covering: (1) the nature and purpose of viewer activity monitoring, (2) assessment of necessity and proportionality, (3) risks to data subjects, (4) measures to mitigate risks. Store the DPIA document in the repository or a linked location.

findings.title: No incident response runbook or breach notification template
findings.description: The infrastructure includes comprehensive monitoring (CloudWatch alarms, SNS alerts, WAF metrics) and daily maintenance runs, but there is no incident response documentation, runbook, or breach notification template in the repository. GDPR Art. 33 requires breach notification to the supervisory authority within 72 hours.
findings.evidence: Monitoring infrastructure exists (SNS alerts, CloudWatch alarms, Lambda error tracking) but no incident response documentation found in the repository.
findings.fix: Create an incident response runbook (docs/incident-response.md) covering: (1) incident classification criteria, (2) escalation procedures, (3) breach notification templates for DPA and affected data subjects, (4) containment and recovery steps, (5) post-incident review process. This is documentation-only and does not require code changes.

findings.title: No rate limiting on authentication or API endpoints
findings.description: There is no rate limiting on any API endpoint, including authentication routes (/api/auth/login, /api/auth/register). The API Gateway stage has no throttling configuration. This enables brute-force password attacks, credential stuffing, and API abuse.
findings.evidence: aws_apigatewayv2_stage 'default' has auto_deploy = true but no default_route_settings with throttling_burst_limit or throttling_rate_limit. The main API Lambda (lambda/api/index.mjs) has no rate limiting logic. Login failures are logged (lines 221, 230) but no counter, delay, or lockout is implemented.
findings.fix: Add throttling to the API Gateway stage: set throttling_burst_limit (e.g., 50) and throttling_rate_limit (e.g., 20/sec) in the default_route_settings. Additionally, implement application-level rate limiting for auth endpoints (e.g., 5 attempts per IP per minute) using DynamoDB-backed counters or AWS WAF rate-based rules.

findings.title: Interpret Lambda has no authentication check
findings.description: The /api/interpret endpoint (Lambda function) processes requests without any JWT token verification. While CORS restricts browser-based cross-origin requests, the API Gateway URL is directly accessible. Anyone who discovers the API Gateway endpoint can send interpretation requests, consuming Anthropic API credits and processing user-like data without authorization.
findings.evidence: The handler function (line 41) parses the event body and dispatches to interpretation handlers without any auth check. Compare to lambda/api/index.mjs line 117: 'const user = await getUser(event); if (!user) return res(401, { error: "Unauthorized" }, origin);' — the interpret Lambda has no equivalent check.
findings.fix: Add JWT verification to the interpret Lambda handler. Import the JWT verification logic from the main API Lambda (or extract to a shared module) and require a valid Bearer token before processing any interpretation request. Pass JWT_SECRET as a Lambda environment variable or use Secrets Manager.

findings.title: Consent withdrawal requires full account deletion
findings.description: The only way to withdraw consent is to delete the entire account. GDPR Art. 7(3) requires that withdrawing consent be as easy as giving it. Users cannot withdraw consent for specific processing activities (e.g., AI interpretations) while retaining their account and data.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 7: 'Withdraw consent: You may withdraw consent at any time by deleting your account.' Settings page (src/app/settings/page.tsx) only offers account deletion — no consent management options.
findings.fix: Add a consent preferences panel in settings allowing users to toggle individual processing activities (e.g., disable AI interpretations, disable analytics). Store consent preferences in the user profile and check them before processing. Update the privacy policy to reflect granular consent withdrawal options.

findings.title: Consent is bundled rather than granular
findings.description: A single checkbox on the signup form covers consent for all processing activities: account creation, data storage, AI interpretation processing, and age confirmation. GDPR requires consent to be specific to each purpose when it is the legal basis.
findings.evidence: <input type='checkbox' required ... /> 'I agree to the Privacy Policy and confirm I am at least 16 years old' — This single checkbox bundles: (1) agreement to privacy policy, (2) consent to all data processing, (3) age confirmation. GDPR Art. 7 conditions require each purpose to have separate, unbundled consent.
findings.fix: Separate the consent checkbox into distinct items: (1) 'I agree to the Privacy Policy', (2) 'I consent to AI-powered interpretations of my readings', (3) 'I confirm I am at least 16 years old'. Store each consent separately with timestamps. Make AI consent optional while the core service can function without it (just don't generate AI interpretations).

findings.title: No mechanism to restrict processing
findings.description: The privacy policy lists the right to restriction of processing (Art. 18), but no functionality exists to restrict processing without deleting the account. Users should be able to request that their data is stored but not actively processed.
findings.evidence: Settings page offers: change name, change email, change password, export data, delete account. No option to pause/restrict/freeze the account or specific processing activities. The only contact mechanism is email (privacy@thenewbeautifulme.com).
findings.fix: Add an 'Account Freeze' or 'Pause Account' feature in settings that sets a flag on the user profile. When frozen, the API should return stored data in read-only mode but not process new readings or send data to Anthropic. This satisfies Art. 18 without requiring full deletion.

findings.title: Email change without verification of new address
findings.description: Users can change their email address to any valid format without verifying ownership of the new address. This undermines data accuracy and could prevent users from exercising data rights if they enter an incorrect email.
findings.evidence: handleChangeEmail (lines 555-596): Takes new email, verifies current password, checks for duplicates, then immediately updates the email in DynamoDB and issues a new JWT. No verification email is sent to the new address. The old email is overwritten with no confirmation.
findings.fix: Implement email verification: (1) On email change request, send a verification link/code to the new email address, (2) Only update the email in DynamoDB after the user confirms via the link/code, (3) Optionally notify the old email of the pending change. This requires an email sending capability (e.g., AWS SES).

findings.title: No reference to Data Processing Agreement with Anthropic
findings.description: User questions and card selections (which may contain personal reflections) are sent to Anthropic's Claude API for processing. The privacy policy discloses Anthropic as a processor but does not reference a Data Processing Agreement (DPA) as required by GDPR Art. 28.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 4: 'Anthropic processes this data under their data processing terms.' This is vague and does not confirm a DPA is in place. GDPR Art. 28(3) requires processing to be governed by a contract/DPA that sets out the subject-matter, duration, nature, and purpose of processing.
findings.fix: Execute a Data Processing Agreement with Anthropic covering the Art. 28 requirements. Update the privacy policy to explicitly state: 'We have a Data Processing Agreement in place with Anthropic governing their processing of your data.' Anthropic offers a DPA through their enterprise/API terms — review and sign it.

findings.title: No mechanism for right to object
findings.description: The privacy policy lists the right to object to processing (Art. 21), but the only available mechanism is contacting an email address. There is no in-app functionality to object to specific processing activities, particularly AI processing of personal data.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 7: 'Object: Object to processing based on legitimate interests.' and 'To exercise these rights, contact privacy@thenewbeautifulme.com.' Settings page has no objection mechanism. While email contact is acceptable, in-app controls would strengthen compliance.
findings.fix: Add toggles in settings for: (1) 'Process my data for AI interpretations' (opt-out stops sending data to Anthropic), (2) 'Include my data in analytics/insights'. When objection is registered, immediately stop the relevant processing. This can reuse the consent preferences infrastructure from COMP-003.

findings.title: CSP allows unsafe-inline for scripts and styles
findings.description: The Content-Security-Policy includes 'unsafe-inline' for both script-src and style-src directives, weakening XSS protection. While frame-ancestors 'none' and other directives provide some mitigation, unsafe-inline allows inline scripts to execute if an XSS vector is found.
findings.evidence: content_security_policy = "...script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline';..." — unsafe-inline for scripts is particularly concerning because the JWT token is stored in localStorage (src/lib/api.ts:9) and could be exfiltrated via XSS.
findings.fix: Replace 'unsafe-inline' with nonce-based or hash-based CSP for scripts. For Next.js static export, use script hashes. For styles, if Tailwind CSS requires unsafe-inline, consider using 'unsafe-hashes' with specific hashes instead. Alternatively, move to a stricter CSP using nonces (requires SSR or a CloudFront Function to inject nonces).

findings.title: JWT token stored in localStorage vulnerable to XSS
findings.description: The authentication token is stored in browser localStorage, which is accessible to any JavaScript running on the page. Combined with the CSP allowing unsafe-inline scripts, this creates a token theft risk via XSS.
findings.evidence: localStorage.getItem('tarot_token') (line 9), localStorage.setItem('tarot_token', token) (line 14). User data also stored: localStorage.setItem('tarot_user', JSON.stringify(user)) (line 35). No HttpOnly or Secure cookie attributes because cookies are not used.
findings.fix: For a static export SPA, localStorage is a common compromise. Mitigate by: (1) Tightening CSP to remove unsafe-inline (see COMP-009), (2) Implementing token rotation (short-lived access tokens with refresh mechanism), (3) Adding token binding to user-agent or IP for anomaly detection. Full mitigation requires moving to HttpOnly cookies (needs server-side rendering or a proxy).

findings.title: No account lockout after failed login attempts
findings.description: There is no mechanism to lock or throttle accounts after repeated failed login attempts. Failed logins are logged but no counter, delay, or lockout is implemented.
findings.evidence: handleLogin (lines 206-237): Failed login attempts log an event (lines 221, 230) with the reason ('unknown_email' or 'wrong_password'), but there is no counter tracking failed attempts per email or IP. No temporary lockout, CAPTCHA challenge, or exponential backoff.
findings.fix: Implement a failed login counter using DynamoDB with TTL. After 5 failed attempts within 15 minutes for the same email, temporarily lock the account for 30 minutes. Log lockout events. Optionally add CAPTCHA (e.g., hCaptcha) after 3 failed attempts.

findings.title: No Cache-Control headers on API responses containing personal data
findings.description: API responses that contain personal data (readings, journals, account info, exports) do not include Cache-Control: no-store headers. This allows browsers and intermediate proxies to cache sensitive personal data.
findings.evidence: The res() helper function (lines 37-43) only sets 'Content-Type: application/json' and CORS headers. No Cache-Control header is included. All endpoints returning personal data (GET /api/readings, /api/journals, /api/export, /api/stats) use this same response helper.
findings.fix: Add 'Cache-Control': 'no-store, no-cache, must-revalidate' to the corsHeaders function or the res() helper for all API responses. This is a one-line change in the response helper.

findings.title: Incomplete audit logging — data operations not logged
findings.description: Authentication events (register, login, password change, account deletion, data export) are logged with structured JSON, but data creation/modification/deletion operations are not logged. This creates gaps in the audit trail.
findings.evidence: Auth events logged: line 202 (register), line 235 (login success), lines 221/230 (login failure), line 551 (password change), line 674 (account delete), line 1046 (data export). NOT logged: handleSaveReading (line 290), handleDeleteReading (line 329), handleSaveJournal (line 422), handleDeleteJournal (line 451), handleShareReading (line 945).
findings.fix: Add structured logging to all data mutation handlers: console.log(JSON.stringify({ event: 'reading_save|delete', userId, readingId, ip, timestamp })). Keep log format consistent with existing auth event logging. Consider a shared logging helper function.

findings.title: No terms of service defining the contractual relationship
findings.description: The privacy policy references 'Contract' as a legal basis for processing (authentication, service provision), but no terms of service document exists to define the contractual relationship. The signup page links only to the privacy policy.
findings.evidence: Consent checkbox: 'I agree to the Privacy Policy and confirm I am at least 16 years old' — references only the privacy policy. No terms of service link. Privacy policy section 3 lists 'Contract' as legal basis for 'Authenticate your account' and 'Store your journal entries and reading history' but no contract terms are defined.
findings.fix: Create a Terms of Service page at src/app/terms/page.tsx covering: service description, entertainment-only disclaimer, user responsibilities, intellectual property, liability limitations, and governing law. Link it from the footer and the registration consent checkbox.

findings.title: Orphaned S3 images when journals expire via DynamoDB TTL
findings.description: Journal entries reference uploaded images stored in S3 via keys (e.g., '{userId}/{uuid}.jpg'). When journals are automatically deleted by DynamoDB TTL after 2 years, the referenced S3 objects remain in the bucket indefinitely. This creates orphaned personal data persisting beyond the stated retention period.
findings.evidence: Journal items store 'images: entry.images || []' (S3 keys) with 'expiresAt: Math.floor(Date.now() / 1000) + (2 * 365 * 24 * 60 * 60)' TTL. No DynamoDB Streams trigger or S3 lifecycle rule exists to clean up S3 objects when journal items expire. Account deletion (handleDeleteAccount, line 622) also does not delete S3 objects.
findings.fix: Implement one of: (1) DynamoDB Streams trigger on TTL deletes that parses image keys and calls S3 DeleteObject, (2) S3 lifecycle rule to expire all objects in the uploads bucket after 2 years, (3) Add S3 object cleanup to the account deletion handler (handleDeleteAccount).

findings.title: Age verification relies solely on self-declaration checkbox
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, CA AADC AB 2273, UK ICO Children's Code
findings.description: The minimum age requirement (16 years) is enforced only by a checkbox during signup that states 'I confirm I am at least 16 years old.' This is trivially bypassable and does not constitute robust age verification under evolving regulations.
findings.evidence: <input type='checkbox' required .../> 'I agree to the Privacy Policy and confirm I am at least 16 years old' — Client-side checkbox only. The backend (lambda/api/index.mjs line 158) checks 'if (!consent)' but this is the consent boolean, not a verified age. No date-of-birth collection, no age estimation, no third-party verification.
findings.fix: Implement a multi-step approach: (1) Replace the checkbox with a date-of-birth entry field during signup, (2) Reject users who enter a DOB indicating age <16, (3) Store DOB only for age calculation (not displayed), (4) Consider integrating an age estimation service if targeting regulated jurisdictions.

findings.title: No parental consent verification mechanism
findings.regulation: GDPR Art. 8, UK ICO Children's Code Standard 2
findings.description: The privacy policy states 'If you are under 16, please do not create an account without parental consent,' but no mechanism exists to verify or collect parental consent. A minor could simply check the age checkbox and create an account.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 9: 'If you are under 16, please do not create an account without parental consent. If we learn that we have collected data from a child under 16 without parental consent, we will delete that data promptly.' No parental consent flow exists in the codebase.
findings.fix: If the service should remain 16+, improve age verification (see COMP-016). If serving users under 16, implement a parental consent flow: (1) Detect under-16 users via DOB, (2) Collect parent/guardian email, (3) Send verification link to parent, (4) Require parent confirmation before account activation.

findings.title: Privacy policy does not identify the lead supervisory authority
findings.description: The privacy policy mentions the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority but does not identify the lead supervisory authority or provide a link to relevant DPA resources.
findings.evidence: Section 7: 'Complaint: Lodge a complaint with your local data protection supervisory authority.' No specific DPA is named. For users unfamiliar with data protection, this is not actionable without more information.
findings.fix: Add a sentence identifying the relevant supervisory authority or providing a link to the EDPB members list for EU users. If the controller has a known establishment, name the lead DPA.

findings.title: Interpret Lambda CORS includes localhost in production
findings.description: The interpret Lambda always includes http://localhost:3000 in its CORS allowed origins list, regardless of environment. The main API Lambda conditionally includes it only in dev mode.
findings.evidence: Interpret Lambda (line 21-25): const ALLOWED_ORIGINS = ['https://thenewbeautifulme.com', 'https://www.thenewbeautifulme.com', 'http://localhost:3000']; — localhost is always included. Compare to main API Lambda (lines 17-22): uses process.env.STAGE === 'dev' conditional.
findings.fix: Apply the same environment-conditional pattern as the main API Lambda: ...(process.env.STAGE === 'dev' ? ['http://localhost:3000'] : []). Add STAGE environment variable to the interpret Lambda's Terraform configuration.

findings.title: JWT secret stored in Lambda environment variable instead of Secrets Manager
findings.description: The JWT signing secret (JWT_SECRET) is passed via a Lambda environment variable, while the Anthropic API key correctly uses AWS Secrets Manager. This inconsistency means the JWT secret is visible in the Lambda console and CloudFormation outputs.
findings.evidence: const JWT_SECRET = process.env.JWT_SECRET; (line 8) — Environment variable. Compare to interpret Lambda: uses aws_secretsmanager_secret for the Anthropic key (terraform/lambda.tf lines 99-111). Lambda env vars are encrypted at rest by AWS, but accessible to anyone with Lambda console access.
findings.fix: Move JWT_SECRET to AWS Secrets Manager alongside the Anthropic key. Update the Lambda to fetch it at cold start. Implement secret rotation by supporting multiple valid secrets during a rotation window.

findings.title: Privacy policy omits retention periods for some data categories
findings.description: The privacy policy lists retention for account data, readings, journals, daily card history, and AI logs. It omits retention details for: custom spreads, card notes, shared reading tokens (90 days TTL), and uploaded images.
findings.evidence: Section 6 lists retention for 4 categories. Missing: custom spreads (indefinite), card notes (indefinite), shared reading tokens (expiresAt: 90 days per lambda/api/index.mjs:976), uploaded images (may persist beyond journal deletion per COMP-015).
findings.fix: Update privacy policy Section 6 to include: 'Custom spreads and card notes: retained until you delete them or your account. Shared reading links: expire after 90 days. Uploaded journal images: retained for 2 years, consistent with journal entry retention.'

findings.title: No breached password checking
findings.description: Password validation only enforces minimum length (10 characters). There is no check against known breached password databases or common password lists.
findings.evidence: Registration (line 157): 'if (password.length < 10)' — only checks length. Password change (line 528): same minimum length check. No complexity requirements or breach database checks.
findings.fix: Add a check against the top 10,000 most common passwords (embed as a static list). Optionally integrate with the HaveIBeenPwned k-Anonymity API to check for breached passwords without exposing the password.

findings.title: No Data Protection Impact Assessment documented
findings.description: The system uses AI (Anthropic Claude) to process personal questions and reflections, producing automated interpretations. This involves new technology processing personal data that could include emotional or psychological information. GDPR Art. 35 may require a DPIA.
findings.evidence: The interpret Lambda sends user questions (which may contain personal reflections, emotional concerns, relationship details) to Anthropic's Claude API. Privacy policy section 11 acknowledges this is 'automated processing that produces personalized guidance.' No DPIA documentation found.
findings.fix: Conduct a DPIA covering: (1) systematic description of AI processing, (2) assessment of necessity and proportionality, (3) assessment of risks to data subjects, (4) measures to address risks. Document as an internal file.

findings.title: No age-appropriate design assessment documented
findings.regulation: CA AADC AB 2273, UK ICO Children's Code
findings.description: No DPIA for child users or age-appropriate design assessment has been documented. While the service targets users 16+, some provisions of the California AADC and UK ICO Children's Code apply to services 'likely to be accessed by children' regardless of target audience.
findings.evidence: No DPIA documents or age-appropriate design assessments found. The service processes personal reflections and emotional data (mood tracking, journal entries) which could be especially sensitive for younger users.
findings.fix: Document a brief age-appropriate design assessment addressing: (1) whether the service is likely to be accessed by under-16s, (2) existing safeguards (age gate, privacy defaults), (3) how emotional/personal data is protected for younger users.

findings.title: No incident response or backup recovery documentation
findings.description: While the infrastructure includes backup capabilities (DynamoDB point-in-time recovery, S3 versioning) and the privacy policy mentions 72-hour breach notification, no incident response procedures, recovery runbooks, or RTO/RPO targets are documented.
findings.evidence: Privacy policy section 10 promises 72-hour breach notification. DynamoDB has point_in_time_recovery enabled (terraform/dynamodb.tf:38). S3 has versioning (terraform/s3.tf:12). But no incident response documentation or recovery procedures found.
findings.fix: Create an incident response document covering: (1) breach detection procedures, (2) escalation contacts, (3) 72-hour notification template for supervisory authority, (4) user notification template, (5) DynamoDB PITR recovery procedure, (6) RTO/RPO targets.
